title: Castile bar soap
subtitle: 100% olive oil, six weeks to a very mild bar
excerpt: A true Castile bar soap: 100% olive oil, no coconut or palm, saponified with sodium hydroxide and cured six weeks. The bar is exceptionally mild, suitable for sensitive and dry skin, though it produces a thin, creamy lather rather than the fluffy bubbles of a coconut-oil bar.
sourceNotes: Anne Bramson, Soap: Making It, Enjoying It (1972), Ch. 2, the traditional Castile formula. Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), Ch. 3, pure olive-oil cure-time note. SoapCalc SAP-value tables (v2024), olive-oil NaOH SAP value 0.135.

content.text: A true Castile bar soap uses 100% olive oil. No coconut, no palm, no added oils. The
saponification

is slow, the cure is long (6-8 weeks), and the resulting bar produces a thin creamy lather rather than the billowy foam of a coconut-oil blend. The reward is exceptional mildness, a bar suitable for dry, sensitive, and baby skin where coconut-heavy bars strip too much.
The lye calculation at 5%
superfat
: 700 g olive oil × 0.135 (NaOH SAP value) = 94.5 g NaOH at 0% superfat. At 5% superfat: 94.5 × 0.95 = 89.8 g NaOH. Working weight: 90 g. Water at 33% of oil weight: 231 g distilled water.
What you need

items.name: Olive oil
items.prepNote: regular refined olive oil. 100% of the oil weight. Extra-virgin is wasted in soap; the lye removes the polyphenols and flavour.

items.name: Distilled water
items.prepNote: distilled or deionised water. 33% of the oil weight. Tap water minerals interfere with the cure.

items.name: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH)
items.prepNote: 99% pure cosmetic-grade NaOH. Calculated at 5% superfat. Weigh to 0.1 g precision.

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Weigh 231 g of distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Weigh 90 g of sodium hydroxide into a small dish. Pour the NaOH into the water in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never the reverse: water into lye flashes violently. The pitcher heats to 80-90°C immediately and fumes briefly; do not lean over it. Stir until the lye has fully dissolved and the solution is clear, then set the pitcher on the heat-safe tray to cool to 38-42°C, which takes 40-60 minutes.
Warm the olive oil

Olive oil is liquid at room temperature, so no melting is needed. Warm the 700 g gently to 38-42°C in a saucepan over a very low heat; it needs only a few minutes. The goal is matching the temperature of the lye solution so both phases combine at the same temperature.
Blend to light trace, pour and mould

When both phases are in the 38-42°C window, pour the lye solution into the olive oil. Lower the stick blender below the surface and pulse on for 10-second bursts, stirring between pulses. Pure olive-oil batter traces very slowly, often 5-10 minutes of blending; this is normal. Watch for
light trace
where a drizzle from the blender holds briefly on the surface. Do not overshoot to thick trace; Castile batter is forgiving to pour even at medium trace.

Pour the batter into the silicone loaf mould in a steady stream. Tap the mould to settle air bubbles. Smooth the surface with the silicone spatula and cover loosely with a tea towel. Leave undisturbed for 48 hours; pure olive-oil batter softens slowly and needs the extra time in the mould before cutting.
Cure: 6-8 weeks
Unmould after 48 hours, cut into bars, and stand on a wire rack with air circulating around all faces. The
cure
for a pure Castile bar is 6-8 weeks minimum; the bars are safe to use at 4 weeks but noticeably harder and milder at 8 weeks, and better still at 12 weeks. The patient batch is worth the wait.
Patch test

Apply a sliver to the inside of the forearm. Wait 24 to 48 hours before regular use. Castile is one of the mildest bar soaps you can make. The olive oil base can still trigger a reaction in people with olive-pollen allergies. If redness appears, stop using the bar.
Storage and shelf life

Two years or more in a cool dark cupboard. Store on a wire rack with air circulating around all faces; airtight plastic traps residual moisture and slows the last stages of cure. The failure mode to watch for is DOS (dreaded orange spots), orange flecks on the bar surface indicating the superfat has gone rancid; the smell changes from clean olive to sharp and paint-like. Castile bars are relatively DOS-resistant because olive oil has a low iodine value, but it can still happen in a warm, damp storage location.
